Pradhan, however, did not directly refer to the ongoing protests - and there was no immediate response to his remarks by the CJP. - Published4 days ago

item.title: Meet the women breaking world records in lumberjack sports
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c6293zk14njo?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
'It makes me different': Meet the women breaking world records in lumberjack sports - Published Karolina Urbanova had never heard of lumberjack sports until a new neighbour moved into her small Czech village and enlisted her help for events when she was a teenager. Now 24, she has just broken two world records at the annual Lumberjack World Championships, which were held over the weekend in Hayward, Wisconsin. The competition, which Hayward has been hosting since 1960, brings people from across America and around the world together to showcase their skills in everything from logrolling, chopping and sawing to speed climbing. Men and women mainly compete separately but some events are mixed. This year, 40 women from across the US and abroad joined 66 male competitors – a far cry from the female representation Urbanova saw when she started out. "There were maybe like three girls doing this sport in Europe," she tells the BBC, adding: "Telling people from the city that you are chopping wood usually every day, they looked weird at me." "It makes me different … so it is what it is." Urbanova was talent-spotted by a male competitor alongside her mother and sister and they began training weekly in a barn - trying out different axes and techniques and getting pointers from more experienced athletes as they entered competitions throughout Europe and further afield. She discovered she was fast, and her new records prove it. In the Women's Standing Block category, where an axe is used to speed-chop through an upright, vertical block of wood, she cut through 9in (22.8cm) of white pine in 19.38 seconds. The previous record was 21.17 seconds. In the Women's Underhand Chop, where competitors stand on a piece of wood and chop between their feet, she cut through 11in of white pine in 21.28 seconds. The fastest previous time was 24.26. Urbanova is perhaps the quintessential example of lumberjack sports' expanding reach, particularly among women. She says that while they are niche in America, they're nearly unheard of in the Czech Republic. That is not the case for Meredith Ingbretson, a Hayward native, who also broke a record at the world championships – winning the Women's Boom Run, where competitors race back and forth across eight linked cedar logs. Her time was 13.78 seconds – less than a second faster than the previous record of 13.98. She said she'd buckled down in the past year - cutting out alcohol and anything else that "didn't align" with achieving her long-held goal. Ingbretson was introduced to lumberjack sports at age three. They are a big part of life in Hayward, which was founded on the timber industry in 1880. It is a place where, as Ingbretson puts it, every resident "has probably been in logrolling lessons at least for one summer". She has now been competing in logrolling for 28 years and began sawing and chopping more recently. The number of female competitors in lumberjack sports has more than trebled in the past 30 years, according to Samantha LaSalle, event director of the Lumberjack World Championships. She's been involved with the sports since she was five, and her six-year-old daughter is already following in her footsteps. While women have taken part in the world championships since the inaugural event in 1960, they were originally limited to logrolling. Getting them entry into chopping and sawing block events as well took time, LaSalle explains. The Women's Standing Block [Chop], for instance, which Urbanova set the new record for, was only added in 2022. "They [women] were saying: 'We can do more. We can do this event that the men have traditionally done'," said LaSalle. "And as more of them practiced and got competitive in it, we said, you know what? There's enough gals that can do this, and do this at a high level, and we need to allow them to do that just like the men do. "Now we're seeing those times drop each year - which just shows you how much better everyone is getting." Both Urbanova and Ingbretson are fuelled by the love of competing and train all year - lifting weights, jogging, doing other cardio and refining their axe skills. They are also drawn by the familial and collegiate atmosphere, as well as the ability to travel and make friends around the world. Prize money is rarely enough to cover all expenses when attending international competitions - the Wisconsin championships paid out $1,000 (£748) to the winners of each category, for example - so Urbanova stayed with a host family in Wisconsin. Ingbretson and her husband, who also competes, were hosted in the Australian state of Tasmania last winter, while her mother put up Canadian and Australian competitors in Hayward for this year's world championships. "I think that's something special … you can go somewhere and they'll let you stay and train you and work with you," Ingbretson said. "It's a very welcoming and open community. It's just so fun to be a part of." She was also bolstered to see women continuing to compete even as their families grow. "There were three or four mums [log]rolling this year, and so I think it's so cool to see that... they're still being competitive rollers or choppers." Word of mouth and exposure - particularly through social media - have played integral roles in promoting the sport with women, organisers and competitors say. "There are a lot of colleges across the US that have timber sports, and they're teaching and getting men and women into the sport of lumberjacking," said Ingbretson, who works as the community education director at Hayward's high school and manages a local fitness centre. She says she's been treated like a celebrity at school and in town this week and finds it particularly gratifying when she's congratulated by young, up-and-coming competitors in the sport. "It's just another reason to be active and engaged in the community that we are in, whether it's bringing in new kids or supporting the kids that just started [log] rolling." Now, she's setting her sights on an all-round title. "To be efficient in multiple disciplines…is very important, and it's very difficult ... it just takes a level of dedication and commitment to make it happen." Urbanova, meanwhile, also hopes her success is inspiring and that she keeps up her record-setting streak. "I'm still growing and still learning, but now the level of the girls is really high, so it's also hard to keep the same rhythm with them because they now want to be better than me," she said. "I just feel pretty proud of myself." Related topics - Published7 days ago
- Published19 March
- Published23 January

item.title: Why Iran is risking so much over the Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cwymx3eevpjo?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Why Iran is risking so much over the Strait of Hormuz - Published After 10 days of exchanging strikes with the US over control of the vital Strait of Hormuz waterway, Iran's leaders may appear to have three choices. None offers a clear route to victory. The first is to accept much of what Washington wants: guarantee free and safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz by ending attacks on commercial shipping, give up or dilute Iran's stockpile of highly enriched uranium, and accept intrusive inspections by the global nuclear watchdog. In return, Tehran would seek an end to the US naval blockade on Iranian ports, sanctions relief, and protection from further attacks. This is probably the least costly option, but also the costliest politically. Reopening Hormuz without securing a recognised Iranian role in managing the waterway would mean surrendering what some in Tehran increasingly see as a newly discovered deterrent, one that may now appear more effective than Iran's missiles, regional proxies, or even its nuclear programme. For the first time, the Islamic Republic has demonstrated its ability to impose immediate costs on the global economy for an attack on its territory by its enemies. The nuclear programme, meanwhile, has long been presented as a right that Iran cannot be forced to abandon. Conceding to demands for tight restrictions on nuclear activities after months of attacks would allow hardliners to argue that American military pressure had worked. It could also open the door to further demands over Iran's missile programme and its relationships with Hezbollah in Lebanon, Iraqi Shia groups and Yemen's Houthis. The second option is escalation. Iran could intensify its attacks on American bases in the region, shipping, and vital infrastructure in Gulf Arab states, hoping that higher energy prices and wider disruption would force Washington and its allies to compromise. This carries the greatest military risk. An attack causing mass casualties or serious damage to Gulf infrastructure could draw Iran's neighbours more directly into the conflict and leave it facing a broader coalition. The third option is to preserve the current instability: enough pressure on shipping and energy markets to retain leverage, but not enough to trigger a return to all-out war with the US. This seems to fit the Islamic Republic's established thinking. Iran does not need to defeat a stronger enemy if it can survive for longer than that enemy is willing to fight. Survival itself can be presented as victory. But sanctions, war damage and inflation are steadily increasing pressure at home. And as time goes on, a paradox may emerge: The more Iran uses the Strait of Hormuz as a weapon, the less powerful it could become if countries find new ways to export energy and other goods out of the region. Parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf has acknowledged the contradiction, arguing that the value of Hormuz lies in increasing the volume of shipping through it rather than reducing it. A permanently unsafe waterway encourages the world to find ways around Iran. - Why the Strait of Hormuz matters so much in the Iran war - Published14 July
Who is making the decisions Complicating matters further is the uncertainty surrounding Iran's decision-making process. Under Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed in a strike at the start of the US-Israeli war with Iran in February, disputes between elected politicians, clerical institutions and the Revolutionary Guards were ultimately settled by the supreme leader. After months of war and under the new leadership of Khamenei's son Mojtaba, that balance of power is less clear. President Masoud Pezeshkian and his diplomats may favour a settlement to ease economic pressure and restore stability. Hardliners in parliament, powerful unelected institutions, and conservative media outlets, meanwhile, may fear that major concessions would undermine the ideological foundations of the Islamic Republic and alienate its core supporters. The biggest question concerns the armed forces, particularly the Revolutionary Guards. Iran's missile programme, regional alliances and strategy in the Strait of Hormuz are central to the Revolutionary Guards' influence, yet it remains unclear how much weight commanders carry in decisions over escalation and negotiations. The three options facing Tehran do not impose the same costs on different parts of the system. Pragmatists may favour a deal, hardliners may prefer endurance, while some military commanders could see escalation as strengthening Iran's bargaining position. The question, therefore, is not only which path Iran chooses, but who has the authority to make that choice. What Iranians are saying The leadership's idea of endurance looks very different to those required to endure it. Iran and the US signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) in June to halt military operations and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, as well as to reach an agreement to end the war over the following 60 days. But three weeks after it was signed, US President Donald Trump declared the ceasefire "over" after Iranian attacks on ships in Hormuz, and US strikes in response. One woman in Iran told BBC Persian that she was "very, very saddened" when the fighting resumed. She had hoped the MoU would give the two sides time to resolve the remaining disputes and restore stability. Instead, she fears the destruction of infrastructure and the deaths of civilians and military personnel. She blamed hardliners inside the Iranian system for imposing destructive policies, while also condemning opposition figures abroad who, in her view, had encouraged war. Another woman in Tehran said the foreign-trade company where she worked had closed, leaving her unemployed for four months despite holding a doctorate and having 17 years of experience. "No-one is hiring because nobody knows what will happen," she said. She described soaring prices, power and water cuts, increased theft, and occasional gunfire at night. She now limits how often she travels around the city. These accounts do not point to a single political conclusion. Foreign attacks can produce anger against the United States and Israel even among opponents of the Islamic Republic. But they also illustrate the limits of asking the public to endure indefinite hardship in the name of resistance. Trump faces difficult choices of his own: widen the war, continue military and economic pressure, or accept a settlement allowing Iran a restricted civilian nuclear programme under stronger supervision. None offers a clear path to victory. The confrontation could continue for months through cycles of attacks, mediation and temporary ceasefires. The decisive question is how long Iran's leaders can preserve Hormuz as leverage without provoking an all-out war, renewed unrest or the construction of alternative routes that make the strait matter less. - Published7 days ago
- Published1 day ago

item.title: Watch: Beluga whales travel by plane and lorry to US from closed Canadian marine park
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/videos/ckg4x8lpe50o?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Watch: Beluga whales travel by plane and lorry to US from closed Canadian marine park Six beluga whales have arrived at aquariums in the United States after being moved from a closed Canadian marine park that could no longer care for the mammals. They are part of a large pod of 30 belugas living at Marineland of Canada that need to be rehomed. Sierra, Acadia, Osiris and Lillooet arrived at a Chicago facility, while Bertie Botts and Frankie arrived at SeaWorld in San Antonio, Texas. Transported in specialised cold-water containers by truck and plane, the animals were accompanied throughout the journey by marine mammal veterinarians. This is the first stage of an unprecedented international relocation effort. The move followed months of planning and veterinary checks, with specialist teams now monitoring the whales as they settle into their new homes. item.title: Watch Texas police officer pull man from burning car
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/videos/clyej920j5po?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Watch Texas police officer pull man from burning car Bodycam and dashcam footage shows a police officer rescue a man seconds before his car was engulfed in flames near Dallas, Texas. Corporal Joshua Swisher was conducting a traffic stop on a motorway when he was alerted to a nearby vehicle emitting smoke. Shortly after directing the vehicle to the emergency lane, flames erupted from underneath it while the driver was still inside. Newly-released footage shows the incident, which happened on 7 June. item.title: Watch: Flooded towns and evacuations as Chile hit by heavy rain
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/videos/cy9wq8j2p7qo?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Watch: Flooded towns and evacuations as Chile hit by heavy rain Officials in Chile have declared a state of catastrophe in the Coquimbo region, and the province of Huasco, after heavy rains triggered floods and mudslides. At least five people are reported to have died, say local mayors. Chile's disaster response service, Senapred, said the extreme weather has left thousands affected, with more than 99,000 people isolated and several people missing nationwide. Areas of Coquimbo recorded historic rainfall, including more than 200mm at Cerro Tololo, according to Chile's meteorological authorities. President José Antonio Kast said the government had decided to activate the constitutional state of catastrophe after evaluating the emergency. item.title: From hundreds to thousands: How India's 'cockroach' protest movement has grown
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/videos/c0qvdywj97ko?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
From hundreds to thousands: How India's 'cockroach' protest movement has grown Thousands of people are attempting to march to India's parliament after the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) called a protest demanding reforms to the education system. The group, which is not a real political party, started out as satire. But on Monday, it held its biggest protest yet. BBC correspondent Azadeh Moshiri, who attended the group's first protest in early June, reports from the ground in Delhi.
